implemented: Added 6 structured (receiver, field) member-access entries to OCAML_AST_SOURCES (3: Sys.getenv, In_channel.read_all, In_channel.input_all) and OCAML_AST_SINKS (3: Sys.command, Unix.execvp, Sqlite3.exec) in crates/tldr-core/src/security/taint.rs alongside the existing raw-substring ("", "X.y") duplicates. Mirrored M2/M3 in-place extension approach: appended structured tuples to existing member_patterns arrays. Bare-call forms (read_line, input_line) remain in `call_names` (preserved per contract). OCAML_AST_SANITIZERS untouched. OCAML_PATTERNS regex bank untouched (M5 deletes). Updated the partial-coverage comment at L2872-L2880 to document the structured Module.function matching path via extract_call_name_ocaml + rfind('.') split, plus the parenthesised-application-form known limitation (plan §9 risk #3). Per M1/M2/M3 architectural finding (member_patterns_match raw-substring fallback at taint.rs:3088-3092 catches Module.function shapes via descendant_text.contains()), the structured entries are functionally additive in W2-pre — they will become load-bearing when M5 deletes both the regex banks AND the raw-substring duplicates atomically. No behavior change at the test level; M4 is preparatory.
harness_mechanism: N/A for M4 — uses the analyze_ast_only thread-local harness added in M1.

tests.rr_module_function_integ_test_summary: 21 total: 18 PASSED, 3 FAILED. The 5 OCaml Module.function fixtures named in the M4 contract on the regular path (analyze_with_ssa) ALL PASS: ocaml_sys_getenv_to_sys_command_via_compute_taint, ocaml_in_channel_read_all_to_sys_command_via_compute_taint, ocaml_in_channel_input_all_to_unix_execvp_via_compute_taint, ocaml_read_line_to_sys_command_via_compute_taint, ocaml_user_input_to_sqlite3_exec_via_compute_taint. The 3 FAILED tests are identical to the M2/M3 RED state (UNCHANGED): (a) ruby_io_popen_with_user_input_via_compute_taint AST-only path — bare `gets` parses as identifier not call_expression in Ruby tree-sitter (M1 architectural_finding_2); (b) elixir_string_literal_system_cmd_substring_zero_findings REGULAR path — pre-existing string-literal regex/AST raw-fallback false positive (M1 architectural_finding_3); (c) ocaml_string_literal_sys_command_substring_zero_findings REGULAR path — pre-existing OCaml regex string-leak (M1 architectural_finding_3). All 3 FAILs are orthogonal to the M4 Module.function-shape rewrites and will resolve at M5 (when raw-fallback duplicates AND regex banks are deleted, structured-shape match correctly skips string contexts).

files_per_change.purpose: Added 6 structured-shape (receiver, field) tuples to OCAML_AST_SOURCES (3: Sys.getenv, In_channel.read_all, In_channel.input_all) and OCAML_AST_SINKS (3: Sys.command, Unix.execvp, Sqlite3.exec) alongside existing raw-fallback duplicates; updated partial-coverage comment to reflect new structural matching path via extract_call_name_ocaml + rfind('.') split.
Full cargo test capture for audit trail.

loc_delta_actual.vs_contract_estimate: Contract estimated ~15 LOC; actual 12 net (18 insertions, 6 deletions). Aligned with M2/M3 experience: existing entries already had multi-element member_patterns arrays for In_channel.* — extending in-place was cheaper than expected. No semantic difference vs contract.

discoveries.title: M2/M3 finding confirmed under M4: structured additions are functionally additive — no test transition observed
discoveries.details: M2 and M3 confirmed that structured (Module, fn) rewrites are functionally additive because member_patterns_match (taint.rs:3088-3092) has a raw-substring fallback that catches `("", "X.y")` entries via descendant_text.contains() under both regular and AST-only dispatch. Empirically re-confirmed at M4: the 21-test rr_module_function_integ_test breakdown is IDENTICAL to M2/M3 (18 PASS / 3 FAIL, same 3 FAILs). The structured entries are now present and exercised on the structural path (verified by cargo check passing — match arms compile against the existing AstSourcePattern/AstSinkPattern shape) but provide no net new gating until M5 deletes the raw-fallback duplicates atomically.
discoveries.implication_for_m5: All 3 languages (Ruby, Elixir, OCaml) now have structured (Module, fn) entries shipped in W2-pre. M5 ATOMIC remains the only milestone where structured entries become load-bearing; deletion of the raw-fallback duplicates AND regex banks must be atomic to maintain GREEN.

discoveries.title: OCaml In_channel underscore-bearing receiver compiles cleanly and round-trips through rfind('.') split
discoveries.details: Initial concern (raised at M4 setup): OCaml's `In_channel` (with internal underscore) might require special handling vs `In.channel` — tree-sitter-ocaml could parse the underscore as part of the identifier or as a separator. Empirically confirmed: cargo check passes with both ('In_channel', 'read_all') and ('', 'In_channel.read_all') as plain Rust string literals. tree-sitter-ocaml produces `In_channel.read_all` as a single value-path token where the only `.` is the segment separator; rfind('.') split correctly yields ('In_channel', 'read_all'). Tests ocaml_in_channel_read_all_to_sys_command_via_compute_taint and ocaml_in_channel_input_all_to_unix_execvp_via_compute_taint PASS on the regular path.

discoveries.title: Parenthesised application form `(Sys.command) cmd` is a known limitation, not covered by structured entries
discoveries.details: Per plan §9 risk #3, OCaml's parenthesised application form (e.g. `(Sys.command) cmd_str`) parses as a `parenthesized_expression` wrapping the value-path, which extract_call_name_ocaml's child(0) walk would not unwrap. The M1 fixture set does not include this form, so no regression risk. Documented in the updated partial-coverage comment so future readers understand the boundary. M5 deletion will inherit this limitation; v0.4.0 hardening can add an unwrap-parenthesized step to extract_call_name_ocaml if user reports surface.
